title: RNN Math, Conceptually
concept: At step t, the hidden state h_t is calculated by combining input x_t and prior state h_{t-1}, passed through an activation function (usually Tanh).

exercises.prompt: Why does an RNN keep a "hidden state" (h) as the correct: false.hidden state (memory state) exist in an RNN?

options.text: It allows the network to remember past inputs
It deletes all previous inputs instantly
It speeds up internet connection speeds

exercises.explain: RNNs have a hidden state that acts as a memory, allowing them to carry information from previous steps forward.

cards.title: Blending Past & Present
cards.body: Have you ever wondered how computers remember the beginning of a sentence when they reach the end? It all comes down to a simple math trick that blends the present with the past!

cards.title: The RNN Math Formula
cards.body: The math behind a Recurrent Neural Network (RNN) state update is simple: it takes the new input (x_t) and the previous memory state (h_{t-1}), adds them together (using weights), and runs them through a Squishing Function (like Tanh) to keep values between -1 and 1. This creates the new memory state (h_t).

cards.title: The Sourdough Starter
cards.body: Imagine baking a loaf of bread. The current state of your dough (h_t) is made by mixing your fresh ingredients (x_t) with the pre-fermented sourdough starter from yesterday (h_{t-1}). You mix them together and bake them in a hot oven (the Tanh activation) to get the final loaf.

cards.title: Tracking a Sentence
cards.body:
Let's look at reading a sentence: "The cat sat". 
At step 1, input is "The", prior state is empty. We calculate state 1.
At step 2, input is "cat". We combine "cat" + state 1 to get state 2.
At step 3, input is "sat". We combine "sat" + state 2 to get state 3.
Each step uses the formula: Current Input + Previous State -> Tanh -> New State.

cards.title: Not So Scary Math
cards.body: Many people think RNNs require complex, heavy calculus equations at every single step to remember things. In reality, it is just a basic addition of the current input and the previous state, scaled by a standard activation function!

cards.title: Inside the State Update
cards.body: See how the current memory state is calculated by combining two different sources of information.

right.text: Combined & squished by Tanh to output New State (h_t)

exercises.prompt: What two main values are combined to calculate the new hidden state (h_t) at step t?

options.text: The current input (x_t) and the previous state (h_{t-1})

exercises.explain: To calculate the current state h_t, the RNN needs the current input x_t and the immediately preceding state h_{t-1}.

exercises.prompt: True or False: The activation function (like Tanh) is used to keep the hidden state values from growing too large.
exercises.explain: This is true. The Tanh activation function squishes the combined inputs so the memory state values always stay between -1 and 1.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing activation function used in the RNN math update:
exercises.sentence: To calculate the next memory state, we combine the input and prior state, then pass them through the [blank] function.
exercises.explain: The Tanh activation function is typically used in RNN state updates to squish the values between -1 and 1, keeping the math stable.

exercises.prompt: Which AI assistant correctly explains the math formula for updating an RNN's state?

options.text: AI A: "The RNN calculates h_t by only looking at the current input x_t and ignoring all previous states to save memory."},{correct:true,id:

exercises.explain: AI B is correct because the standard RNN math combines the current input (x_t) and the previous hidden state (h_{t-1}) using a Tanh activation function to generate the new state (h_t).

exercises.prompt: A developer is building a system to predict stock prices using an RNN. At time step t, how does the RNN update its hidden memory state (h_t)?

options.text: It combines the input (x_t) with the prior state (h_{t-1}) and applies a Tanh activation.
It discards the prior state (h_{t-1}) and only runs the activation on the new input (x_t).
It multiplies the new input (x_t) by a random number without using any activation.
It averages all past states without looking at the current input (x_t).

exercises.explain: At any step t, an RNN updates its memory state (h_t) by taking both the new incoming data (x_t) and the previous memory state (h_{t-1}), combining them, and scaling the result through the Tanh activation function.
